---
title: Research
listing:
  - id: research
    type: grid
    grid-columns: 2
    fields: [title, image, description, categories]
    image-height: 300px
    field-links: [title]
    contents:
      - title: "Neurophysiological Markers of Existential Experience"
        description: >
          How does the brain process the awareness of mortality — and what happens 
          when that processing shifts? At the Contemplative Neuroscience and 
          Neurophenomenology Lab (University of Haifa, PI: Prof. Aviva 
          Berkovich-Ohana), I analyze EEG time-series to extract neurophysiological 
          markers of death anxiety and belief updating. The theoretical framework is 
          predictive processing and active inference: beliefs are not discrete 
          propositions but probability distributions encoded in a hierarchical 
          generative model, and transformative experience involves a genuine 
          reconfiguration of those priors. I use visual mismatch negativity (vMMN) 
          paradigms and time-frequency decomposition to trace how the brain 
          anticipates, violates, and updates its models of self and world.
        categories: [Contemplative Neuroscience, EEG, Predictive Processing, Altered States]
        image: ""
        path: ""

      - title: "Tree Responses to Climatic Variability: Eco-Hydrological Modeling"
        description: >
          How do trees respond to the interplay of soil moisture deficits and 
          atmospheric drought — and what do long-term monitoring datasets reveal 
          about adaptive capacity across decades of climate change? In Yair Mau's 
          Eco-Hydrology Lab (Hebrew University of Jerusalem), I apply data science 
          methods to Long-Term Ecological Research (LTER) datasets in Israel, 
          building eco-hydrological models of tree growth, contraction, and recovery 
          under climatic stress. The work connects empirical monitoring trajectories 
          to mechanistic models of water uptake and carbon dynamics, asking not just 
          what models predict but what observed tree communities actually reveal 
          about resilience and vulnerability.
        categories: [Eco-Hydrology, Complex Systems, Climate, Dryland Ecology]
        image: ""
        path: ""

      - title: "Spatial Self-Organization and Resilience in Dryland Ecosystems"
        description: >
          Dryland vegetation does not distribute itself randomly — it self-organizes 
          into spatial patterns (spots, stripes, gaps) that function as a collective 
          buffer against aridity. My Ph.D. research at Ben-Gurion University (PI: 
          Prof. Ehud Meron) used reaction-diffusion modeling, bifurcation analysis, 
          and numerical continuation to study how these patterns form, what dynamical 
          signatures precede ecosystem collapse, and how spatial organization shapes 
          both short-term resilience and long-term evolutionary adaptation. A key 
          finding: the same self-organized configurations that stabilize a community 
          under stress can constrain its capacity to adapt when the environment 
          changes faster than selection can respond.
        categories: [Pattern Formation, Dryland Ecology, Nonlinear Dynamics, Complex Systems]
        image: ""
        path: ""

      - title: "Spreading Phenomena on Complex Networks"
        description: >
          How does the topology of a social or biological network shape whether a 
          perturbation cascades or gets absorbed? During my postdoctoral position at 
          Tel Aviv University, I built large-scale hybrid epidemiological models to 
          simulate disease spreading in structured populations, contributing to 
          network-based analyses of epidemic propagation during the COVID-19 pandemic 
          in Israel. A key insight from this work: in sociotechnical systems, behavior 
          is not a fixed parameter but a dynamic variable that co-evolves with the 
          system state — network topology shapes spread, but spread reshapes topology 
          through behavioral adaptation.
        categories: [Network Science, Epidemiology, Complex Systems, Computational Modeling]
        image: ""
        path: ""

      - title: "Knowledge Graphs and LLM Agents for Complex Domains"
        description: >
          Between academic positions, I worked as a data scientist and R&D lead 
          building knowledge graph systems and LLM-based agentic pipelines across 
          domains including precision agriculture, psychophysiological health, and 
          carbon sequestration. This industry experience deepened my interest in how 
          structured knowledge representations can make AI systems more interpretable, 
          grounded, and aligned with domain expertise — questions I now bring back 
          into research contexts, including ongoing work on ecological knowledge 
          graphs connecting long-term monitoring data to policy-relevant stakeholder 
          decisions.
        categories: [Knowledge Graphs, LLM Agents, Data Science, AI]
        image: ""
        path: ""
---

:::{#research}
:::
